audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel / Shemuel Aleph chapter 29, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Aphek/Jezreel geography, Philistine military organization, Hebrew label, Achish's defense of David, commanders' security concern, victory-song memory, YHWH oath, David's ambiguous loyalty language, and dismissal at dawn.

verses.restored: Philistines gathered all their camps at Aphek, and Israel was camping by spring that is in Jezreel.
Lords of Philistines were passing by hundreds and by thousands, and David and his men were passing at rear with Achish.

Commanders of Philistines said, What are these Hebrews? Achish said to commanders of Philistines, Is this not David, servant of Saul king of Israel, who has been with me these days or these years? I have found nothing against him from day of his falling away until this day.

Commanders of Philistines were angry against him. Commanders of Philistines said to him, Return the man, and let him return to his place where you appointed him. He shall not go down with us into battle, so that he does not become an adversary to us in battle. With what could this one make himself acceptable to his lord? Is it not with heads of these men?
Is this not David of whom they answered in dances, saying, Saul struck his thousands, and David his ten thousands?

Achish called David and said to him, As YHWH lives, you are upright, and your going out and your coming in with me in camp is good in my eyes, because I have not found evil in you from day you came to me until this day. Yet in eyes of lords you are not good.
Now return and go in peace, and do not do evil in eyes of lords of Philistines.
David said to Achish, But what have I done, and what have you found in your servant from day I was before you until this day, that I may not come and fight against enemies of my lord the king?
Achish answered and said to David, I know that you are good in my eyes like messenger of Divine. Nevertheless commanders of Philistines have said, He shall not go up with us into battle.
Now rise early in morning, you and servants of your lord who came with you. Rise early in morning, and when it is light for you, go.
David rose early, he and his men, to go in morning to return to land of Philistines, and Philistines went up to Jezreel.

translation_notes: Philistine commanders' distrust arises in a specific wartime coalition and from David's known history. It may not justify modern ethnic profiling, collective suspicion, or calling refugees security threats.
Achish's oath by YHWH shows religious language crossing community boundaries; it does not establish that his judgment of David is accurate.
David's words remain strategically ambiguous in context. The chapter does not reveal whether he intended to fight Israel, defect in battle, or preserve his cover.
Coalition commanders set a military boundary against Achish's patronage decision. Shared leadership can restrain a powerful sponsor, though the setting remains preparation for war.
David's exclusion protects him from immediate participation in the battle where Saul and Jonathan will die, but it does not erase his prior massacres or deception.
The phrase 'these Hebrews' belongs to ancient identity politics and must not become a modern slur or antisemitic category.
safety_note: Do not use this chapter to stigmatize migrants, refugees, dual nationals, or minority soldiers as inherently disloyal. Do not portray strategic ambiguity as simple holiness. The scene is a coalition-security dispute inside an unjust war system, not a template for ethnic exclusion.
